summary: Charity Majors argues that the phrase 'breaking down silos' is a meaningless cliché that substitutes for actual critical thinking about organizational communication problems. She contends that real solutions to collaboration problems are complex and specific, and people who use vague buzzphrases almost never have the hands-on experience of actually solving these problems.
key_points: The phrase 'breaking down silos' tells you nothing about what someone actually did or what problems they solved
More communication isn't automatically better — too much can be as harmful as too little
What matters is the quality and structure of communication: who talks to whom, when, and why
Business clichés like 'breaking down silos' and 'fix your culture' are substitutes for critical thinking
People who have actually done the hard work of improving organizations talk about the specific work, not buzzphrases
money_quotes: You can't just blindly throw 'more communication' at your teams. Too much communication can be just as much of a problem and a burden as too little.
When someone says they are 'breaking down silos', whether in an interview, a panel, or casual conversation, it tells me jack shit about what they actually did.
Cliches are a substitute for critical thinking.

People who have done the hard labor of building better organizations with healthy communication flows, less inefficiency, and alignment around a single mission — people who have gotten all the people rowing in the same direction — tend to talk about the work. People who haven't, say they were 'breaking down silos.'
key_insight: Organizational buzzphrases like 'breaking down silos' are red flags that signal someone lacks the specific, hard-won experience of actually diagnosing and solving complex communication and collaboration problems.
